meta.title: Onboarding: First 30 Minutes
meta.subject: A developer's first 30 minutes with a new framework — where they feel curious, where they grind, where they quit

scenes.title: First 30 Minutes
scenes.tagline: Where a developer feels curious — and where they quit
scenes.footnote: A demo of the new journey-map primitive   ·   one person, six stages, one arc

beats.narration: This is what the first thirty minutes with a new framework actually feel like, from the inside.
We are not narrating the API. We are tracing the person walking through it -- where curiosity holds, where it breaks, and the exact touchpoint that broke it.
Six stages, one arc, one developer. The shape teams keep missing when they audit their docs by reading them instead of by walking them.

scenes.heading: Six stages — one developer, thirty minutes

journeyStages.label: Install
painPoints: no SHA pinned in the docs

journeyStages.label: Configure
painPoints: six rotating examples, none of them current
the v2 -> v3 rename buried in a changelog

journeyStages.label: First bug
journeyStages.sub: 0:21 — a stack trace from inside the framework
painPoints: the trace stops at framework code, not user code
the StackOverflow answer is for v1

journeyStages.label: Switch tabs
touchpoints: the comparison post bookmarked at minute zero
painPoints: nothing in the last ten minutes felt addressable

beats.narration: Minute zero: discovery. A tweet, a screencast, a comparison post. The curiosity is high -- that is what got the developer here.
Two minutes in: install. The one-line installer works. Confidence climbs. There is no SHA pinned in the docs -- a small thing, but the supply-chain reflex flickers.
Six minutes in: hello world. Paste, save, hot-reload. This is the peak of the arc. The framework has just shown the developer it can do the thing they came for.

Fourteen minutes in: configure. Auth, a database, an env-var dance. The configuration guide has six examples and the v2 to v3 rename is buried in a changelog. The arc breaks -- fatigue, not failure, but fatigue.
Twenty-one minutes in: the first bug. The stack trace stops at framework code, not user code. The first StackOverflow answer is for v1. The arc is now well below the bad line.

Twenty-nine minutes in: Command-T. The developer opens a competitor's docs. The framework did not lose them at install or at hello-world. It lost them at minute fourteen, in a configuration guide that drifted out of date.

scenes.heading: The drop is not where teams think

beats.narration: Here is the trade-off the team made without realizing it. They audit the installer and the hello-world -- the bright minutes -- because those are the pages new developers see first.
The cliff is not there. The cliff is minute fourteen -- the configuration guide that drifted, and the framework stack trace that does not point at user code. The two are the same fatigue, compounded.
Chose: audit the docs the team is proud of. Costs: the developer quits in a section nobody reads on purpose. The configuration guide is unowned -- and unowned is where every onboarding arc breaks.

scenes.heading: Where the arc actually breaks
points: Disposition — the framework does not lose developers at install. It loses them at minute fourteen, in an unowned configuration guide.
The single biggest residual risk — the framework's stack trace stops at framework code, not user code. Every developer's first bug is a debugger that points at someone else's mistake.
What to watch — the time-to-quit metric, not the time-to-hello-world. A team that optimises hello-world while config rots is auditing the wrong stage.

beats.narration: The disposition: this framework does not lose developers at install. It loses them at minute fourteen, in a configuration guide nobody owns.

The residual risk: the framework's stack trace stops at framework code, not user code. Every developer's first bug is a debugger that points at someone else's mistake -- and that is the moment they switch tabs.
Watch the time-to-quit, not the time-to-hello-world. A team that optimises hello-world while config rots is reviewing the wrong stage of the journey.
